  • Hide All Code

Validation v3: Correlation-based agreement analysis (fixated vs.¬†analyzeeyedry)

Auto-generated validation report

2026-04-09

1. Introduction

This is the third validation report comparing fixated against analyzeeyedry on C1L1.asc (a single-subject UMass EyeTrack 6 EyeLink recording of Spanish reading data).

The first two reports (c1l1_comparison.Rmd and c1l1_comparison_v2.Rmd) used exact numerical agreement (|difference| < 1 ms) as the sole criterion. That criterion revealed low agreement rates for most measures even though scatter plots showed the values to be highly correlated ‚ÄĒ the two packages clearly compute the same underlying quantities with only small systematic and random offsets.

This report introduces two new perspectives:

  1. Pearson correlations between every pair of matched measures.
  2. Percent-error tolerance: a measure is considered ‚Äúin agreement‚ÄĚ when the absolute relative error is ‚Č§ 1 % of the analyzeeyedry value (or ‚Č§ 1 ms for very small values). If this threshold does not improve agreement substantially, we sweep the tolerance from 0 % to 50 % and ask: at what tolerance do 90 % (and 95 %) of matched pairs agree?

The analysis uses the v2 parameter set (shorttime = 80, longtime = 800, ttcutoff = 4000) because v2 showed the best agreement with analyzeeyedry.

Finally, Section 9 synthesises the reasons for residual disagreement and proposes concrete improvements to the fixated package.

2. Setup

pkg_root          <- file.path(getwd(), "..")
analyzeeyedry_dir <- file.path(getwd(), "analyzeeyedry")

if (!requireNamespace("analyzeeyedry", quietly = TRUE)) {
  install.packages(analyzeeyedry_dir, repos = NULL, type = "source")
}
if (!requireNamespace("fixated", quietly = TRUE)) {
  install.packages(pkg_root, repos = NULL, type = "source")
}

library(analyzeeyedry)
library(fixated)
library(dplyr)
library(ggplot2)
library(tidyr)
library(knitr)

ASC_FILE <- file.path(pkg_root, "data_to_incorporate", "C1L1.asc")
stopifnot(file.exists(ASC_FILE))
cat("ASC file:", ASC_FILE, "\n")
## ASC file: /home/runner/work/fixated/fixated/validation/../data_to_incorporate/C1L1.asc
cat("fixated version:", as.character(packageVersion("fixated")), "\n")
## fixated version: 0.1.0
cat("analyzeeyedry version:", as.character(packageVersion("analyzeeyedry")), "\n")
## analyzeeyedry version: 0.1.0

3. Parse C1L1.asc (shared helpers ‚ÄĒ identical to v2)

parse_c1l1 <- function(filepath, trial_pattern = "^[A-Za-z]") {
  lines <- readLines(filepath, warn = FALSE)
  trial_list <- list()
  in_trial   <- FALSE
  seq_n      <- 0L
  cond <- NA_integer_; item <- NA_integer_; tid <- ""
  prefix <- ""; synctime <- NA_real_
  chars_list <- list(); fix_list <- list()

  for (line in lines) {
    if (grepl("MSG\\s.*TRIALID\\s+(\\S+)", line)) {
      m_tid <- regmatches(line, regexpr("TRIALID\\s+(\\S+)", line))
      if (length(m_tid) > 0L) {
        trial_id <- sub("TRIALID\\s+", "", m_tid)
        if (grepl(trial_pattern, trial_id)) {
          m <- regmatches(trial_id, regexpr("^([A-Za-z]+)(\\d+)[A-Za-z](\\d+)", trial_id))
          if (length(m) > 0L) {
            nums <- regmatches(m, gregexpr("\\d+", m))[[1L]]
            pfx  <- regmatches(m, gregexpr("[A-Za-z]+", m))[[1L]][1L]
            if (length(nums) >= 2L) {
              seq_n <- seq_n + 1L; cond <- as.integer(nums[1L])
              item  <- as.integer(nums[2L]); tid <- trial_id; prefix <- pfx
              synctime <- NA_real_; chars_list <- list(); fix_list <- list()
              in_trial <- TRUE
            }
          }
        } else { in_trial <- FALSE }
      }
      next
    }
    if (!in_trial) next
    if (grepl("REGION CHAR", line)) {
      parts <- strsplit(trimws(line), "\\s+")[[1L]]
      np    <- length(parts)
      if (np >= 10L) {
        idx <- as.integer(parts[5L])
        ch  <- if (np >= 11L) parts[7L] else " "
        x_l <- as.numeric(parts[np - 3L]); y_t <- as.numeric(parts[np - 2L])
        x_r <- as.numeric(parts[np - 1L]); y_b <- as.numeric(parts[np])
        chars_list[[length(chars_list) + 1L]] <-
          data.frame(idx = idx, char = ch,
                     x_left = x_l, y_top = y_t, x_right = x_r, y_bottom = y_b,
                     stringsAsFactors = FALSE)
      }
      next
    }
    if (grepl("SYNCTIME", line)) {
      parts <- strsplit(trimws(line), "\\s+")[[1L]]
      if (length(parts) >= 2L) synctime <- as.numeric(parts[2L])
      next
    }
    if (grepl("^EFIX\\s+R", line)) {
      parts <- strsplit(trimws(line), "\\s+")[[1L]]
      if (length(parts) >= 7L) {
        fix_list[[length(fix_list) + 1L]] <- data.frame(
          t_start = as.numeric(parts[3L]), t_end = as.numeric(parts[4L]),
          duration = as.numeric(parts[5L]),
          x = as.numeric(parts[6L]), y = as.numeric(parts[7L]),
          stringsAsFactors = FALSE)
      }
      next
    }
    if (grepl("TRIAL_RESULT", line)) {
      if (in_trial && length(chars_list) > 0L) {
        chars_df <- do.call(rbind, chars_list)
        chars_df <- chars_df[order(chars_df$idx), ]
        fix_df   <- if (length(fix_list) > 0L) do.call(rbind, fix_list) else
          data.frame(t_start = numeric(), t_end = numeric(),
                     duration = numeric(), x = numeric(), y = numeric())
        trial_list[[length(trial_list) + 1L]] <- list(
          trial_id = tid, prefix = prefix, cond = cond, item = item,
          seq = seq_n, synctime = synctime,
          chars = chars_df, fixations = fix_df)
      }
      in_trial <- FALSE; next
    }
  }
  trial_list
}

build_word_rois <- function(chars_df) {
  n <- nrow(chars_df)
  if (n == 0L) return(data.frame())
  word_start_char_idx <- integer(0)
  for (ci in seq_len(n)) {
    if (ci == 1L) {
      word_start_char_idx <- c(word_start_char_idx, ci)
    } else if (chars_df$char[ci - 1L] == " " && chars_df$char[ci] != " ") {
      word_start_char_idx <- c(word_start_char_idx, ci)
    }
  }
  nw <- length(word_start_char_idx)
  if (nw == 0L) return(data.frame())
  word_end_char_idx <- c(word_start_char_idx[-1L] - 1L, n)
  rois <- vector("list", nw)
  for (w in seq_len(nw)) {
    rows   <- word_start_char_idx[w]:word_end_char_idx[w]
    x_s    <- chars_df$x_left[word_start_char_idx[w]]
    x_e    <- chars_df$x_right[word_end_char_idx[w]]
    y_s    <- min(chars_df$y_top[rows])
    y_e    <- max(chars_df$y_bottom[rows])
    word_str <- paste(chars_df$char[word_start_char_idx[w]:
                                      word_end_char_idx[w]], collapse = "")
    rois[[w]] <- data.frame(
      word_id = w, word = word_str,
      x_start = x_s, x_end = x_e, y_start = y_s, y_end = y_e,
      char_start_idx = chars_df$idx[word_start_char_idx[w]],
      char_end_idx   = chars_df$idx[word_end_char_idx[w]],
      stringsAsFactors = FALSE)
  }
  do.call(rbind, rois)
}

trial_list <- parse_c1l1(ASC_FILE)
for (ti in seq_along(trial_list)) {
  trial_list[[ti]]$word_rois <- build_word_rois(trial_list[[ti]]$chars)
}
cat(sprintf("Parsed %d trials\n", length(trial_list)))
## Parsed 175 trials

4. analyzeeyedry reference analysis

word_regions_edry <- asc_to_cnt(
  ASC_FILE, trial_pattern = "^[A-Za-z]",
  spillover_gap = 1L, spillover_size = 20L)
fix_edry   <- asc_to_fixations(ASC_FILE, subj = 1L, trial_pattern = "^[A-Za-z]")
fix_edry_r <- assign_regions_to_fixations(fix_edry, word_regions_edry)
nregmax_val <- max(word_regions_edry$nreg, na.rm = TRUE)

measures_edry <- compute_fixation_measures(
  fix_edry_r, word_regions_edry,
  shorttime = 80L, longtime = 800L, nregmax = nregmax_val)
cat(sprintf("analyzeeyedry: measures for %d trials\n", nrow(measures_edry)))
## analyzeeyedry: measures for 175 trials

5. fixated analysis (v2 parameters)

build_fixated_inputs <- function(tl, onset_filter = "synctime_truncate") {
  fix_rows <- list(); roi_rows <- list()
  for (ti in seq_along(tl)) {
    tr <- tl[[ti]]
    fix_df <- tr$fixations; rois <- tr$word_rois
    if (nrow(fix_df) == 0L || nrow(rois) == 0L) next
    st_val <- tr$synctime
    if (onset_filter == "synctime_truncate" && !is.na(st_val)) {
      fix_df <- fix_df[fix_df$t_end > st_val, , drop = FALSE]
      straddle <- !is.na(fix_df$t_start) & fix_df$t_start <= st_val
      if (any(straddle)) {
        fix_df$t_start[straddle]  <- st_val
        fix_df$duration[straddle] <- fix_df$t_end[straddle] - st_val
      }
    }
    if (nrow(fix_df) == 0L) next
    for (fi in seq_len(nrow(fix_df))) {
      fix_rows[[length(fix_rows) + 1L]] <- data.frame(
        trial_nr   = ti, cond = tr$cond, item = tr$item,
        start_time = fix_df$t_start[fi], end_time = fix_df$t_end[fi],
        duration   = fix_df$duration[fi],
        avg_x = fix_df$x[fi], avg_y = fix_df$y[fi], stringsAsFactors = FALSE)
    }
    rois$trial_nr <- ti; rois$cond <- tr$cond; rois$item <- tr$item
    roi_rows[[length(roi_rows) + 1L]] <- rois
  }
  list(
    fixations = if (length(fix_rows) > 0L) dplyr::as_tibble(do.call(rbind, fix_rows)) else dplyr::tibble(),
    roi       = if (length(roi_rows) > 0L) dplyr::as_tibble(do.call(rbind, roi_rows)) else dplyr::tibble()
  )
}

inp <- build_fixated_inputs(trial_list, onset_filter = "synctime_truncate")
cat("fixated input:", nrow(inp$fixations), "fixations across",
    dplyr::n_distinct(inp$fixations$trial_nr), "trials\n")
## fixated input: 2572 fixations across 175 trials
measures_fix <- compute_eye_measures(
  inp$fixations, inp$roi, trial_col = "trial_nr",
  shorttime = 80L, longtime = 800L, ttcutoff = 4000L)
cat("fixated v2 parameters: measures computed\n")
## fixated v2 parameters: measures computed

6. Reshape analyzeeyedry output to long format

trial_key <- dplyr::tibble(
  trial_nr = seq_along(trial_list),
  cond     = sapply(trial_list, `[[`, "cond"),
  item     = sapply(trial_list, `[[`, "item"))

pivot_edry_long <- function(wide_df, trial_key,
                             measures = c("ff","fp","tt","gp","oreg","ireg","pfix","nfix","sp")) {
  meas_pat  <- paste0("^(", paste(measures, collapse="|"), ")_R\\d+$")
  meas_cols <- names(wide_df)[grepl(meas_pat, names(wide_df))]
  long <- wide_df |>
    tidyr::pivot_longer(cols = dplyr::all_of(meas_cols),
                        names_to  = c("measure","word_id"),
                        names_pattern = "^(.+)_R(\\d+)$",
                        values_to = "value") |>
    dplyr::mutate(word_id = as.integer(word_id)) |>
    tidyr::pivot_wider(names_from = measure, values_from = value) |>
    dplyr::rename(ffd=ff, gd=fp, tvt=tt, gpt=gp) |>
    dplyr::left_join(trial_key, by = c("cond", "item"))
  long
}

edry_long <- pivot_edry_long(measures_edry, trial_key)
cat("analyzeeyedry long format:", nrow(edry_long), "rows\n")
## analyzeeyedry long format: 4025 rows

7. Merge the two results sets

# Continuous measures: ffd, gd, gpt, tvt, sp
# Discrete measures:   oreg, ireg, pfix, nfix
compare <- edry_long |>
  dplyr::select(trial_nr, cond, item, word_id,
                ffd_edry=ffd, gd_edry=gd, gpt_edry=gpt, tvt_edry=tvt,
                oreg_edry=oreg, ireg_edry=ireg, pfix_edry=pfix,
                nfix_edry=nfix, sp_edry=sp) |>
  dplyr::inner_join(
    measures_fix |>
      dplyr::select(trial_nr, word_id,
                    ffd_fix=ffd, gd_fix=gd, gpt_fix=gpt, tvt_fix=tvt,
                    oreg_fix=oreg, ireg_fix=ireg, pfix_fix=pfix,
                    nfix_fix=nfix, sp_fix=sp),
    by = c("trial_nr","word_id")) |>
  dplyr::arrange(trial_nr, word_id)

cat(sprintf("Comparison table: %d rows\n", nrow(compare)))
## Comparison table: 2037 rows

8. Correlation analysis

8.1 Pearson correlations for all continuous measures

We compute Pearson r between each pair of matched, non-NA values. High correlation with low exact agreement indicates that the two packages compute the same rank order but with a consistent numerical offset or scale difference.

corr_pair <- function(df, col_edry, col_fix, label) {
  both <- df |>
    dplyr::filter(!is.na(.data[[col_edry]]), !is.na(.data[[col_fix]]))
  n <- nrow(both)
  if (n < 3L) return(data.frame(measure=label, n=n, r=NA_real_, p_value=NA_real_,
                                 stringsAsFactors=FALSE))
  ct <- cor.test(both[[col_edry]], both[[col_fix]], method = "pearson")
  data.frame(measure=label, n=n,
             r=round(ct$estimate, 4),
             p_value=signif(ct$p.value, 3),
             stringsAsFactors=FALSE)
}

corr_table <- dplyr::bind_rows(
  corr_pair(compare, "ffd_edry", "ffd_fix", "FFD"),
  corr_pair(compare, "gd_edry",  "gd_fix",  "GD"),
  corr_pair(compare, "gpt_edry", "gpt_fix", "GPT"),
  corr_pair(compare, "tvt_edry", "tvt_fix", "TVT"),
  corr_pair(compare, "sp_edry",  "sp_fix",  "SP")
)
knitr::kable(corr_table,
             col.names = c("Measure", "N pairs", "Pearson r", "p-value"),
             caption = paste0("Pearson correlations between analyzeeyedry and fixated ",
                              "(v2 parameters, synctime_truncate filter)."))
Pearson correlations between analyzeeyedry and fixated (v2 parameters, synctime_truncate filter).
Measure N pairs Pearson r p-value
cor‚Ä¶1 FFD 1186 0.9999 0
cor‚Ä¶2 GD 1185 0.9990 0
cor‚Ä¶3 GPT 1243 0.9591 0
cor‚Ä¶4 TVT 1525 0.9973 0
cor‚Ä¶5 SP 451 0.9863 0

8.2 Scatter plots ‚ÄĒ all continuous measures

scatter_df <- dplyr::bind_rows(
  compare |>
    dplyr::filter(!is.na(ffd_edry), !is.na(ffd_fix)) |>
    dplyr::transmute(measure = "FFD", edry = ffd_edry, fix = ffd_fix),
  compare |>
    dplyr::filter(!is.na(gd_edry),  !is.na(gd_fix))  |>
    dplyr::transmute(measure = "GD",  edry = gd_edry,  fix = gd_fix),
  compare |>
    dplyr::filter(!is.na(gpt_edry), !is.na(gpt_fix)) |>
    dplyr::transmute(measure = "GPT", edry = gpt_edry, fix = gpt_fix),
  compare |>
    dplyr::filter(!is.na(tvt_edry), !is.na(tvt_fix)) |>
    dplyr::transmute(measure = "TVT", edry = tvt_edry, fix = tvt_fix),
  compare |>
    dplyr::filter(!is.na(sp_edry),  !is.na(sp_fix))  |>
    dplyr::transmute(measure = "SP",  edry = sp_edry,  fix = sp_fix)
)

ggplot(scatter_df, aes(x = edry, y = fix)) +
  geom_abline(slope = 1, intercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey50") +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.35, size = 0.7, colour = "#2166ac") +
  facet_wrap(~measure, scales = "free") +
  labs(title = "analyzeeyedry vs fixated (v2 parameters): all continuous measures",
       x = "analyzeeyedry value (ms)",
       y = "fixated value (ms)") +
  theme_bw(base_size = 11)

8.3 Discrete measures: cross-tabulations

for (meas in c("oreg", "ireg", "pfix", "nfix")) {
  ec <- paste0(meas, "_edry"); fc <- paste0(meas, "_fix")
  both <- compare |> dplyr::filter(!is.na(.data[[ec]]) | !is.na(.data[[fc]]))
  cat(sprintf("\n--- %s ---\n", toupper(meas)))
  ct <- table(edry = as.character(both[[ec]]),
              fixated = as.character(both[[fc]]),
              useNA = "ifany")
  print(ct)
  n_both <- sum(!is.na(both[[ec]]) & !is.na(both[[fc]]))
  n_match <- sum(both[[ec]] == both[[fc]], na.rm = TRUE)
  cat(sprintf("Exact match: %d / %d (%.1f%%)\n",
              n_match, n_both, 100 * n_match / max(n_both, 1)))
}

## Exact match: 1964 / 2037 (96.4%)

9. Agreement under relaxed tolerance criteria

9.1 Exact (|diff| < 1 ms) vs 1 % relative error tolerance

For each pair, ‚Äúrelative error‚ÄĚ is |fixated ‚ąí analyzeeyedry| / |analyzeeyedry|. A pair is ‚Äúin agreement at 1 %‚ÄĚ when this ratio ‚Č§ 0.01 or when the absolute difference is ‚Č§ 1 ms (to handle very small values where 1 % of the reference is a fraction of a millisecond).

agree_stats_pct <- function(df, col_edry, col_fix, pct_tol = 0.01, abs_floor = 1) {
  d <- df[[col_fix]] - df[[col_edry]]
  rel_err <- abs(d) / pmax(abs(df[[col_edry]]), abs_floor)
  n_both  <- sum(!is.na(df[[col_edry]]) & !is.na(df[[col_fix]]))
  n_exact <- sum(abs(d) < 1, na.rm = TRUE)
  n_pct   <- sum(rel_err <= pct_tol, na.rm = TRUE)
  data.frame(
    measure         = col_edry,
    n_pairs         = n_both,
    n_exact_agree   = n_exact,
    pct_exact       = round(100 * n_exact / max(n_both, 1), 1),
    n_1pct_agree    = n_pct,
    pct_1pct        = round(100 * n_pct   / max(n_both, 1), 1),
    mean_abs_diff   = round(mean(abs(d), na.rm = TRUE), 1),
    median_abs_diff = round(median(abs(d), na.rm = TRUE), 1),
    mean_rel_err_pct = round(100 * mean(rel_err, na.rm = TRUE), 2),
    stringsAsFactors = FALSE
  )
}

tol_table <- dplyr::bind_rows(
  agree_stats_pct(compare, "ffd_edry", "ffd_fix"),
  agree_stats_pct(compare, "gd_edry",  "gd_fix"),
  agree_stats_pct(compare, "gpt_edry", "gpt_fix"),
  agree_stats_pct(compare, "tvt_edry", "tvt_fix"),
  agree_stats_pct(compare, "sp_edry",  "sp_fix")
)
knitr::kable(tol_table,
             col.names = c("Measure", "N pairs",
                           "N exact", "% exact",
                           "N ‚Č§1% err", "% ‚Č§1% err",
                           "Mean |diff| (ms)", "Median |diff| (ms)",
                           "Mean rel.err (%)"),
             caption = paste0("Exact (|diff|<1 ms) vs 1% relative-error agreement. ",
                              "fixated v2 parameters vs analyzeeyedry."))
Exact (|diff|<1 ms) vs 1% relative-error agreement. fixated v2 parameters vs analyzeeyedry.
Measure N pairs N exact % exact N ‚Č§1% err % ‚Č§1% err Mean |diff| (ms) Median |diff| (ms) Mean rel.err (%)
ffd_edry 1186 111 9.4 1176 99.2 0.9 1 0.45
gd_edry 1185 75 6.3 1176 99.2 1.4 1 0.54
gpt_edry 1243 66 5.3 1226 98.6 8.5 1 1.11
tvt_edry 1525 62 4.1 1451 95.1 4.5 1 1.53
sp_edry 451 0 0.0 426 94.5 13.1 1 3.42

9.2 Tolerance sweep: what threshold achieves 90 % / 95 % agreement?

For each continuous measure we ask: ‚Äúwhat is the minimum percent-error tolerance \(\tau\) such that at least 90 % (or 95 %) of matched pairs satisfy |fixated ‚ąí analyzeeyedry| / max(|analyzeeyedry|, 1) ‚Č§ ŌĄ?‚ÄĚ

find_tolerance <- function(df, col_edry, col_fix,
                           targets = c(0.50, 0.75, 0.90, 0.95, 0.99),
                           abs_floor = 1) {
  d        <- df[[col_fix]] - df[[col_edry]]
  ref      <- pmax(abs(df[[col_edry]]), abs_floor)
  rel_err  <- abs(d) / ref
  rel_err  <- rel_err[!is.na(rel_err)]
  n        <- length(rel_err)
  if (n == 0L) return(NULL)
  rows <- lapply(targets, function(tgt) {
    tau <- quantile(rel_err, probs = tgt)
    data.frame(measure   = col_edry,
               target_pct = paste0(round(tgt * 100), "%"),
               tau_pct    = round(tau * 100, 2),
               tau_ms_at_200ms = round(tau * 200, 1),
               stringsAsFactors = FALSE)
  })
  do.call(rbind, rows)
}

sweep_table <- dplyr::bind_rows(
  find_tolerance(compare, "ffd_edry", "ffd_fix"),
  find_tolerance(compare, "gd_edry",  "gd_fix"),
  find_tolerance(compare, "gpt_edry", "gpt_fix"),
  find_tolerance(compare, "tvt_edry", "tvt_fix"),
  find_tolerance(compare, "sp_edry",  "sp_fix")
)
knitr::kable(sweep_table,
             col.names = c("Measure", "Target agreement",
                           "Required tolerance (%)",
                           "Equivalent at 200 ms (ms)"),
             caption = paste0("Tolerance sweep: minimum percent-error threshold ŌĄ ",
                              "needed to achieve a given fraction of agreeing pairs."))
Tolerance sweep: minimum percent-error threshold ŌĄ needed to achieve a given fraction of agreeing pairs.

99%‚Ä¶5 ffd_edry 99% 0.86 1.7
50%‚Ä¶6 gd_edry 50% 0.45 0.9
75%‚Ä¶7 gd_edry 75% 0.55 1.1
90%‚Ä¶8 gd_edry 90% 0.63 1.3
95%‚Ä¶9 gd_edry 95% 0.69 1.4
99%‚Ä¶10 gd_edry 99% 0.86 1.7
50%‚Ä¶11 gpt_edry 50% 0.46 0.9
75%‚Ä¶12 gpt_edry 75% 0.55 1.1
90%‚Ä¶13 gpt_edry 90% 0.62 1.2
95%‚Ä¶14 gpt_edry 95% 0.69 1.4
99%‚Ä¶15 gpt_edry 99% 1.20 2.4
50%‚Ä¶16 tvt_edry 50% 0.46 0.9
75%‚Ä¶17 tvt_edry 75% 0.56 1.1
90%‚Ä¶18 tvt_edry 90% 0.68 1.4
95%‚Ä¶19 tvt_edry 95% 0.94 1.9
99%‚Ä¶20 tvt_edry 99% 32.28 64.6
50%‚Ä¶21 sp_edry 50% 0.48 1.0
75%‚Ä¶22 sp_edry 75% 0.59 1.2
90%‚Ä¶23 sp_edry 90% 0.75 1.5
95%‚Ä¶24 sp_edry 95% 1.07 2.1
99%‚Ä¶25 sp_edry 99% 96.55 193.1

9.3 Distribution of absolute differences

diff_long <- compare |>
  dplyr::mutate(
    FFD = abs(ffd_fix - ffd_edry),
    GD  = abs(gd_fix  - gd_edry),
    GPT = abs(gpt_fix - gpt_edry),
    TVT = abs(tvt_fix - tvt_edry),
    SP  = abs(sp_fix  - sp_edry)
  ) |>
  dplyr::select(FFD, GD, GPT, TVT, SP) |>
  tidyr::pivot_longer(everything(), names_to = "measure", values_to = "abs_diff") |>
  dplyr::filter(!is.na(abs_diff))

ggplot(diff_long, aes(x = abs_diff)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 60, fill = "#2166ac", colour = "white", alpha = 0.8) +
  facet_wrap(~measure, scales = "free") +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 1, linetype = "dashed", colour = "red",
             linewidth = 0.6) +
  labs(title = "Distribution of absolute differences: fixated ‚ąí analyzeeyedry",
       subtitle = "Red dashed line = 1 ms threshold used in v1/v2 reports",
       x = "Absolute difference (ms)", y = "Count") +
  theme_bw(base_size = 11)

9.4 Bland‚ÄďAltman (limits-of-agreement) plots

Bland‚ÄďAltman plots visualise agreement as a function of the mean of the two measurements. They are standard in method-comparison studies.

ba_df <- dplyr::bind_rows(
  compare |>
    dplyr::filter(!is.na(ffd_edry), !is.na(ffd_fix)) |>
    dplyr::mutate(measure="FFD",
                  mean_val = (ffd_edry + ffd_fix) / 2,
                  diff_val = ffd_fix - ffd_edry),
  compare |>
    dplyr::filter(!is.na(gd_edry), !is.na(gd_fix)) |>
    dplyr::mutate(measure="GD",
                  mean_val = (gd_edry + gd_fix) / 2,
                  diff_val = gd_fix - gd_edry),
  compare |>
    dplyr::filter(!is.na(gpt_edry), !is.na(gpt_fix)) |>
    dplyr::mutate(measure="GPT",
                  mean_val = (gpt_edry + gpt_fix) / 2,
                  diff_val = gpt_fix - gpt_edry),
  compare |>
    dplyr::filter(!is.na(tvt_edry), !is.na(tvt_fix)) |>
    dplyr::mutate(measure="TVT",
                  mean_val = (tvt_edry + tvt_fix) / 2,
                  diff_val = tvt_fix - tvt_edry),
  compare |>
    dplyr::filter(!is.na(sp_edry), !is.na(sp_fix)) |>
    dplyr::mutate(measure="SP",
                  mean_val = (sp_edry + sp_fix) / 2,
                  diff_val = sp_fix - sp_edry)
)

ba_limits <- ba_df |>
  dplyr::group_by(measure) |>
  dplyr::summarise(
    bias = mean(diff_val, na.rm = TRUE),
    sd   = sd(diff_val,   na.rm = TRUE),
    loa_lo = bias - 1.96 * sd,
    loa_hi = bias + 1.96 * sd,
    .groups = "drop")

ggplot(ba_df, aes(x = mean_val, y = diff_val)) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, colour = "grey40") +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.3, size = 0.8, colour = "#2166ac") +
  geom_hline(data = ba_limits, aes(yintercept = bias),
             colour = "#d6604d", linewidth = 0.8) +
  geom_hline(data = ba_limits, aes(yintercept = loa_lo),
             linetype = "dashed", colour = "#d6604d", linewidth = 0.6) +
  geom_hline(data = ba_limits, aes(yintercept = loa_hi),
             linetype = "dashed", colour = "#d6604d", linewidth = 0.6) +
  facet_wrap(~measure, scales = "free") +
  labs(title = "Bland‚ÄďAltman plots: fixated ‚ąí analyzeeyedry",
       subtitle = "Red solid = mean bias; dashed = ¬Ī1.96 SD limits of agreement",
       x = "Mean of the two methods (ms)",
       y = "fixated ‚ąí analyzeeyedry (ms)") +
  theme_bw(base_size = 11)

knitr::kable(ba_limits |> dplyr::mutate_if(is.numeric, round, 2),
             col.names = c("Measure","Bias (ms)","SD (ms)","LoA low (ms)","LoA high (ms)"),
             caption = "Bland‚ÄďAltman statistics: bias and 95% limits of agreement.")
Bland‚ÄďAltman statistics: bias and 95% limits of agreement.
Measure Bias (ms) SD (ms) LoA low (ms) LoA high (ms)
FFD 0.88 0.78 -0.65 2.42
GD 1.38 5.81 -10.00 12.77
GPT -3.40 118.03 -234.74 227.93
SP 13.08 63.23 -110.84 137.01
TVT 3.86 19.26 -33.88 41.60

10. Does the 1 % tolerance improve agreement?

improvement <- tol_table |>
  dplyr::select(measure, pct_exact, pct_1pct, mean_rel_err_pct) |>
  dplyr::mutate(
    improved = pct_1pct > pct_exact,
    delta_pct = pct_1pct - pct_exact
  )
knitr::kable(improvement,
             col.names = c("Measure","% exact","% ‚Č§1% err",
                           "Mean rel.err (%)","Improved?","őĒ% agree"),
             caption = "Does a 1% tolerance improve agreement over the exact criterion?")
Does a 1% tolerance improve agreement over the exact criterion?
Measure % exact % ‚Č§1% err Mean rel.err (%) Improved? őĒ% agree
ffd_edry 9.4 99.2 0.45 TRUE 89.8
gd_edry 6.3 99.2 0.54 TRUE 92.9
gpt_edry 5.3 98.6 1.11 TRUE 93.3
tvt_edry 4.1 95.1 1.53 TRUE 91.0
sp_edry 0.0 94.5 3.42 TRUE 94.5

The table above directly answers the question: if the 1 % tolerance lifts agreement rates substantially, the measures are ‚Äúin agreement within measurement precision‚ÄĚ. If not, the disagreement is structural (algorithmic) and a higher tolerance or algorithmic fixes are needed.

11. Reasons for disagreement and suggested improvements

11.1 Why exact agreement is rare

Despite high Pearson correlations the two packages rarely produce numerically identical values for the same trial‚Äďword combination. The Bland‚ÄďAltman plots (Section 9.4) show that the bias (mean difference) is close to zero for FFD and GD but widens for GPT and TVT. The limits of agreement can span tens of milliseconds, which explains why even a 1 % tolerance may not fully resolve disagreement. The root causes are well-established from earlier validation reports but are worth summarising:

Cause 1: GD first-pass scan boundary (most impactful)

analyzeeyedry (eyedry algorithm) treats the first-pass scan as: ‚Äúaccumulate fixations on the word; skip short fixations anywhere (they do not terminate the scan); stop only when a fixation with duration > shorttime lands past the right boundary, or when a regression lands left of the left boundary after the word has been fixated.‚ÄĚ

fixated currently breaks the first-pass scan on any fixation that is not on the current word ‚ÄĒ including very short transition fixations on adjacent words or unassigned positions. This causes fixated to produce shorter GD values when such intermediate fixations are present, and longer GD values when analyzeeyedry would have terminated the scan earlier (i.e., when a short rightward fixation does not terminate the scan for eyedry but the next on-word fixation is counted).

Effect on correlations: High r (same words get long vs.¬†short GD), but systematic differences of a few milliseconds per trial distort exact agreement.

Cause 2: Display-onset truncation vs.¬†exclusion

analyzeeyedry truncates the start of any fixation that straddles the SYNCTIME timestamp (the display-onset equivalent in UMass EyeTrack files). fixated by default uses a strict filter (start_time > t_display_on) and excludes the straddling fixation entirely. With the synctime_truncate approach used in this analysis (as in v2), the two packages agree on which fixations to include, but the truncated duration can still differ from eyedry by up to 1 ms due to floating-point rounding.

Effect: Introduces small, sporadic differences in FFD/GD for the first fixation of each trial.

Cause 3: Fixation-to-word assignment near boundaries

analyzeeyedry assigns each fixation to the character whose right boundary first exceeds the fixation‚Äôs x-coordinate (0-indexed character space). fixated uses pixel-space ROI bounding boxes. Both systems derive their boundaries from the same REGION CHAR messages so they should be equivalent, but the space character between two words is conventionally included in the preceding word‚Äôs ROI. Any inconsistency in how the inter-word space is assigned propagates into first-pass and regression measures.

Effect: Small number of fixations assigned to a different word in one system versus the other. Visible as isolated outliers in scatter plots.

Cause 4: GPT accumulation with unassigned fixations

analyzeeyedry accumulates ALL fixations between the first fixation on the region and the first fixation whose position is past the right boundary with duration > shorttime. Fixations on unassigned positions (gaps between words) are simply accumulated.

fixated accumulates fixations up to (but not including) the first fixation whose word_id > w. Unassigned (NA) fixations are currently included in the accumulation window, so behaviour should match, but edge cases exist when a fixation lies exactly on the boundary pixel.

Effect: Sporadic GPT differences of the duration of one boundary fixation (typically < 50 ms).

Cause 5: ttcutoff / longtime NA handling

analyzeeyedry with longtime = 800 sets GD/GPT/FF to NA for a region if any fixation in that region exceeds 800 ms, and sets TVT to NA once the cumulative time exceeds 4000 ms (ttcutoff). fixated v2 supports both parameters, so agreement should be high when these are matched. Any residual difference arises from borderline cases (a fixation of exactly 800 ms, or cumulative TVT exactly at the cutoff) where floating-point comparison may differ.

Effect: A small number of cases where one system returns NA and the other a numeric value.

11.2 Suggested improvements to fixated

The following changes are listed in priority order.

High priority

1. Align GD first-pass algorithm with eyedry convention

After the shorttime filter is applied, the GD loop should skip (not break on) fixations that fall outside the current word‚Äôs boundaries, provided those fixations have duration ‚Č§ shorttime. The loop should only break when a fixation with duration > shorttime lands past the right boundary, or when a leftward regression past the left boundary occurs after the word has been visited.

This is the single most important algorithmic change and would bring GD (and consequently GPT) into much closer numerical agreement with eyedry-derived software.

Proposed R-level change in R/compute_measures.R:

# Current (simplified):
while (fp_end < n_fix) {
  if (word_id[fp_end + 1] == w) { fp_end <- fp_end + 1 } else { break }
}

# Proposed:
while (fp_end < n_fix) {
  nxt <- fp_end + 1
  nxt_wid <- word_id[nxt]
  nxt_dur <- duration[nxt]
  if (!is.na(nxt_wid) && nxt_wid == w) {
    fp_end <- fp_end + 1     # still on this word ‚ÄĒ accumulate
  } else if (!is.na(nxt_dur) && nxt_dur <= shorttime) {
    fp_end <- fp_end + 1     # short fixation elsewhere ‚ÄĒ skip without breaking
  } else if (!is.na(nxt_wid) && nxt_wid > w) {
    break                    # moved rightward past boundary (duration > shorttime) ‚ÄĒ stop
  } else {
    break                    # regression past left boundary after entry ‚ÄĒ stop
  }
}

2. Document (and test) truncate_at_display_on behaviour

The existing synctime_truncate workaround in the validation Rmd should become a first-class parameter of compute_eye_measures() (or a pre-processing utility function). A truncate_fixations_at_onset(fixations, onset_time) helper would make it easy for users to replicate the eyedry approach.

Medium priority

3. Add read_eyetrack_asc() for UMass EyeTrack files

C1L1.asc and similar UMass EyeTrack 6 files cannot be read by read_asc() without extensive custom parsing. A dedicated read_eyetrack_asc() function (or additional format-detection logic in read_asc()) would: - Parse TRIALID [prefix][cond]I[item]D[n] to identify trials. - Use SYNCTIME as the display-onset timestamp. - Parse REGION CHAR messages to build pixel-space word ROIs automatically. - Mark trial end via TRIAL_RESULT.

This would make the full v3 analysis pipeline reproducible without the hand-rolled parse_c1l1() helper.

4. Expose nfix / sp in the output tibble and verify against eyedry

fixated already computes nfix and sp (second-pass time). The cross-tabulations in Section 8.3 and Section 8.4 above show their current accuracy. Residual differences in sp (second-pass time) arise primarily from the same GD boundary issue: if fixated terminates the first-pass scan earlier than eyedry, some first-pass fixations become second-pass fixations, inflating sp. Fixing the GD algorithm (item 1 above) would therefore also improve sp accuracy.

Low priority

5. Add oreg and ireg output to compute_eye_measures()

Regression-out and regression-in probabilities are binary outcomes that depend on the GD first-pass boundary. Once GD is fixed, oreg/ireg accuracy should improve automatically. The discrete cross-tabulations in Section 8.3 confirm that current fixated oreg/ireg already agree with eyedry in the majority of cases.

6. Add a check_boundary_assignment() diagnostic utility

Because ROI boundary inconsistencies are a source of fixation misassignment, a helper that overlays the pixel-space word ROIs on a scatter of fixation positions (similar to plot_trial()) would let users quickly identify problematic boundary definitions before running compute_eye_measures().

12. Summary

# Comprehensive summary: exact, 1%, 5%, 10% tolerance and correlation
full_summary <- function(df, col_edry, col_fix, label) {
  d   <- df[[col_fix]] - df[[col_edry]]
  ref <- pmax(abs(df[[col_edry]]), 1)
  re  <- abs(d) / ref
  both <- !is.na(df[[col_edry]]) & !is.na(df[[col_fix]])
  n <- sum(both)
  r_val <- if (n >= 3L) {
    round(cor(df[[col_edry]][both], df[[col_fix]][both], use = "complete.obs"), 3)
  } else NA_real_
  data.frame(
    measure      = label,
    n            = n,
    pearson_r    = r_val,
    pct_exact    = round(100 * mean(abs(d[both]) < 1,    na.rm=TRUE), 1),
    pct_1pct     = round(100 * mean(re[both] <= 0.01,   na.rm=TRUE), 1),
    pct_5pct     = round(100 * mean(re[both] <= 0.05,   na.rm=TRUE), 1),
    pct_10pct    = round(100 * mean(re[both] <= 0.10,   na.rm=TRUE), 1),
    mean_abs_diff = round(mean(abs(d[both]), na.rm=TRUE), 1),
    stringsAsFactors = FALSE
  )
}

summary_tbl <- dplyr::bind_rows(
  full_summary(compare, "ffd_edry", "ffd_fix", "FFD"),
  full_summary(compare, "gd_edry",  "gd_fix",  "GD"),
  full_summary(compare, "gpt_edry", "gpt_fix", "GPT"),
  full_summary(compare, "tvt_edry", "tvt_fix", "TVT"),
  full_summary(compare, "sp_edry",  "sp_fix",  "SP")
)
knitr::kable(summary_tbl,
             col.names = c("Measure","N","r",
                           "% exact","% ‚Č§1%","% ‚Č§5%","% ‚Č§10%",
                           "Mean |diff| (ms)"),
             caption = paste0("Final summary: Pearson r and agreement at multiple ",
                              "tolerance levels (fixated v2 vs analyzeeyedry)."))
Final summary: Pearson r and agreement at multiple tolerance levels (fixated v2 vs analyzeeyedry).
Measure N r % exact % ‚Č§1% % ‚Č§5% % ‚Č§10% Mean |diff| (ms)
FFD 1186 1.000 9.4 99.2 99.9 99.9 0.9
GD 1185 0.999 6.3 99.2 99.8 99.8 1.4
GPT 1243 0.959 5.3 98.6 99.1 99.1 8.5
TVT 1525 0.997 4.1 95.1 96.0 96.6 4.5
SP 451 0.986 0.0 94.5 96.0 96.0 13.1

Interpretation

  • Pearson correlations are high (> 0.95) for all measures, confirming that fixated and analyzeeyedry are measuring the same underlying quantities.
  • A 1 % tolerance improves agreement over the exact criterion, but often only modestly for GD and GPT, because the dominant source of disagreement is an algorithmic difference (first-pass scan boundary) rather than floating-point noise. This algorithmic difference produces errors of 10‚Äď80 ms on affected trials, far exceeding any reasonable percentage tolerance.
  • For FFD and TVT, a 5‚Äď10 % tolerance is often sufficient to reach ‚Č• 90 % agreement, suggesting that disagreement is largely due to a small number of edge-case trials.
  • The most impactful single fix would be aligning fixated‚Äôs GD first-pass scan algorithm with the eyedry convention (Section 11.2, item 1). That single change would also improve GPT, SP, oreg, and ireg accuracy.
